explanation: The requirement stipulates that in either PCV or PSV mode, IF the Expiratory Pause is set by the GUI ('expiratoryPauseButton'), then after the end of the next expiratory phase ('expiratoryPhaseEnd'), the ventilator (1) checks that Expiratory Pause is still required ('expiratoryPause'), and (2) both the inspiratory and expiratory valves close ('inValveClose' and 'outValveClose'), maintaining this state until Expiratory Pause is stopped from the GUI. The template chosen expresses that always (globally), if we are in PCV or PSV mode AND the GUI sets Expiratory Pause AND we've just exited expiration, THEN both valves will close as long as Expiratory Pause is active. Here, 'bool_exp1' (PCVMode | PSVMode) represents being in ventilating modes; 'bool_exp2' captures the logic for 'expiratoryPauseButton & expiratoryPhaseEnd'; and 'bool_exp3' expresses 'expiratoryPause & inValveClose & outValveClose', i.e., while pause is required, both valves are closed. We set N_DURATION=1 to ensure reactiveness after the next expiration, but further fine-tuning to N_DURATION may be needed depending on timing requirements and implementation.

The property states that whenever either PCVMode or PSVMode is active and the Expiratory Pause is set by the GUI (expiratoryPauseButton), then when Expiratory Pause is required (expiratoryPause), both the inspiratory and expiratory valves (inValveClose and outValveClose) should be closed. This uses a global invariant implication template, where the condition is that PCV or PSV is active and Expiratory Pause is required, and the response is that both valves are closed. This simplification captures the key safety property: in the specified modes, if Expiratory Pause is required, both valves must be closed until the pause is released.

This property expresses: If the system is in PCV or PSV mode and the Expiratory Pause is set by the GUI (expiratoryPauseButton), then starting from the end of the next expiration phase (expiratoryPhaseEnd), if the pause is still required (expiratoryPause is true), both inspiratory and expiratory valves (inValveOpen, outValveOpen) remain closed until the pause is stopped by the GUI. The boolean expressions do not include temporal operators and refer only to atomic propositions and boolean logic. 

Here, bool_exp1 captures 'expiratoryPauseEnd & (PCVMode | PSVMode) & expiratoryPauseButton', which represents the moment after the next expiration and while still requesting pause in the correct mode. The implication leads to exp3: when the pause is still requested, both valves must be closed.
N_DURATION is set to 1 as the requirement is immediate after the next expiratory phase end.

This LTL property formalizes the following logic: In either PCV or PSV mode, when the Expiratory Pause is requested by the GUI ('expiratoryPauseButton'), the system should wait for the end of the current expiration phase ('expiratoryPhaseEnd'). If the requirement for Expiratory Pause ('expiratoryPause') is still active, then both the inspiratory and expiratory valves should close ('!inValveOpen & !outValveOpen') and this state persists until the Expiratory Pause is deactivated by the GUI. The template selected ensures that whenever the Expiratory Pause is set by the GUI, after the end of the next expiration phase, then as long as Expiratory Pause remains active, the valves remain closed, meeting the natural language requirement that the pause can be initiated at this time and maintained until stopped by the GUI.

This property formalizes the following requirements:
- In either PCVMode (Pressure Controlled Ventilation) or PSVMode (Pressure Support Ventilation), if an Expiratory Pause is set by the GUI (expiratoryPauseButton), the ventilator should, after the end of the current expiration phase (expiratoryPhaseEnd), and while Expiratory Pause is still required (expiratoryPause), close both the inspiratory (inValveClose) and expiratory (outValveClose) valves until Expiratory Pause ends.

This is best expressed as: Whenever we are in (PCVMode or PSVMode) and expiratoryPauseButton is pressed, then after the next expiratoryPhaseEnd event, if expiratoryPause is still active, then inValveClose and outValveClose must both be held true (valves closed) until Expiratory Pause is stopped.

We choose template 17 (G (bool_exp2 -> (G bool_exp3))), which suits a 'whenever condition, always response' specification. Here, the condition (bool_exp2) is (PCVMode | PSVMode) & expiratoryPauseButton, and the response (bool_exp3) is (expiratoryPause -> (inValveClose & outValveClose)), which ensures that whenever the mode is PCV or PSV, and Expiratory Pause is requested, while Expiratory Pause is active, both valves stay closed.

This LTL property captures the requirement that when the ventilator is in either PCV or PSV mode, and if the Expiratory Pause button has been set by the GUI, then after the end of the next expiration phase, if the Expiratory Pause is still requested, both the inspiratory and expiratory valves should close. The property uses 'PCVMode' or 'PSVMode' to select the two modes, and checks if 'expiratoryPauseButton' (Expiratory Pause requested by the GUI) is set. The property then ensures that after the next 'expiratoryPhaseEnd', if 'expiratoryPause' is still set (Expiratory Pause still required), both 'inValveClose' and 'outValveClose' will be set simultaneously (both valves closed). The formula uses a bounded eventually modality to enforce that both valves are closed within a specified duration (N_DURATION) after the completion of the expiration phase and Expiratory Pause being required.

This specification is about ensuring that when an Expiratory Pause is set by the GUI in PCV or PSV mode, after the next expiration phase ends, both valves (inspiratory and expiratory) close and remain so until the pause is stopped. To formalize this, we need to say that in PCV or PSV mode, once expiratoryPauseButton is pressed (by the GUI), then after the next expiration phase ends (expiratoryPhaseEnd), the expPause should start (expiratoryPause), and while expiratoryPause remains true, both inValveOpen and outValveOpen should remain false (i.e., valves stay closed), until expiratoryPauseButton is cleared (pause stopped by GUI). While LTL is not able to directly express the 'wait for next expiration to end' causality in a basic temporal implication, a robust approximation is that whenever (PCVMode OR PSVMode) AND expiratoryPauseButton hold, then eventually (after the next expiration end) there will be a period during which expiratoryPause is true and both inValveOpen and outValveOpen remain false until the pause is deactivated. Using the available atomic propositions, the most direct template to encode this is by stating: Always, if (PCVMode OR PSVMode) AND expiratoryPauseButton AND expiratoryPhaseEnd, then globally (expiratoryPause -> (!inValveOpen & !outValveOpen)). This guarantees that whenever an expiratory pause is requested in relevant modes, and following the next end of expiration, if the pause is in effect, both valves must remain closed.

This property expresses: Whenever the system is in either PCVMode or PSVMode and the Expiratory Pause is set via the GUI (expiratoryPauseButton is true), then after the next expiration phase ends (expiratoryPhaseEnd), as long as the Expiratory Pause remains active (expiratoryPause is true), both inspiratory and expiratory valves stay closed (inValveOpen == false and outValveOpen == false). This is encoded so that at all times, if we are in PCV or PSV and Expiratory Pause has been set, then whenever expiratoryPhaseEnd and expiratoryPause are true together, both valves must be closed. This captures both the mode, the trigger, the waiting for next expiration end, and the requirement to keep both valves closed for the duration of the pause after it is set.

The requirement specifies that when the ventilator is in PCV or PSV mode and the Expiratory Pause is set by the GUI, after the next expiration phase, both the inspiratory and expiratory valves must close as long as the Expiratory Pause is still required (i.e., commanded by the GUI), and this should persist until the Expiratory Pause is stopped by the GUI. This can be captured as: whenever (1) PCVMode or PSVMode is active, and (2) the Expiratory Pause is set by the GUI (expiratoryPauseButton), then (after the expiration phase ends) both inValveClose and outValveClose should be true as long as expiratoryPause is asserted, i.e., during the expiratoryPause period. This is a strong safety property: 'whenever in PCV or PSV mode and ExpiratoryPauseButton is set, if expiratoryPause holds, both valves should be closed.' This maps to template 10: G (bool_exp2 -> bool_exp3), where bool_exp2 identifies the precondition (PCVMode or PSVMode) & expiratoryPauseButton, and bool_exp3 specifies the safety condition (expiratoryPause -> (inValveClose & outValveClose)). N_DURATION is not directly required here, so is set to null.

This property expresses that whenever the ventilator is operating in PCV or PSV mode AND the GUI has set the Expiratory Pause AND the current breathing phase has just ended expiration (expiratoryPhaseEnd), then in the next step if the Expiratory Pause request is still present, both the inspiratory and expiratory valves must close. The formula uses the fact that after (PCVMode | PSVMode) & expiratoryPauseButton & expiratoryPhaseEnd becomes true, the next state (X) must have both inValveClose and outValveClose as well as continued expiratoryPauseButton request. No second boolean expression (bool_exp2) is required for template 41, so it is set to a trivially true constant (to satisfy the parser as a boolean expression), e.g. "True".
